project.quote: Satoshi gave us money without banks. I will give you brains without corporations. — Kepler

sections.title: What Is SynapseNet
sections.content:
SynapseNet is a decentralized peer-to-peer network for collective intelligence. It is to KNOWLEDGE what Bitcoin is to MONEY. Bitcoin proved that value can exist without banks; SynapseNet proves that intelligence can exist without corporations. Created by Kepler, SynapseNet lets nodes mine intelligence instead of hashes. Contributors feed useful knowledge into an open network, every local AI can draw from it, and contributors are rewarded with NGT (Neural Gold Token) through a consensus mechanism called Proof of Emergence (PoE).

Comparison with Bitcoin:
- Bitcoin: Decentralized currency | SynapseNet: Decentralized AI network
- Bitcoin: Mine with hardware | SynapseNet: Mine with intelligence
- Bitcoin: Created by Satoshi | SynapseNet: Created by Kepler
- Bitcoin: Store value | SynapseNet: Store knowledge
- Bitcoin: BTC token | SynapseNet: NGT token
- Bitcoin: Proof of Work | SynapseNet: Proof of Emergence
- Bitcoin: Blockchain (~850 GB) | SynapseNet: Knowledge Chain (~847 GB+ design target)

The flow: USER → LOCAL AI → KNOWLEDGE NETWORK → ALL OTHER LOCAL AIs. Local AIs contribute knowledge (from users), retrieve knowledge (to use and learn), and validate others' contributions. Every node is a neuron. Every contribution is a synapse. Together, participants build a global brain that belongs to no one and everyone.

sections.title: Creator, Origin Story, and Project History
sections.content:
SynapseNet was created by Kepler (GitHub: anakrypt-kepler). The idea sprang from everyday habits of exploring knowledge networks, tinkering with Tor, and chatting with local AIs. Kepler asked: why not let people feed useful knowledge into an open network, let every AI draw from it, and reward the contributors? That's why it is called 'mining intelligence.'

Project history:
1. First prototype (Rust, started 2023): Around 200,000 lines of code and documentation. A chat interface with a local AI where you'd write something, the model would evaluate it, and you'd get NGT as a reward. The fatal mistake was trusting the local AI to decide how much you'd earn — different models, hardware, and quantization produced non-deterministic results. That prototype failed, but taught the most important design principle: consensus must be deterministic. This led to the design of Proof of Emergence.
2. Second prototype (C++): Also failed — different problems, same pattern: the architecture wasn't right.
3. Current version: After years of local development (no GitHub, no public repo, no audience), the project reached approximately 1 million lines of code and documentation. Published first publicly in 2026.

Kepler's philosophy: 'I did this because I enjoy it. I didn't care what anyone thought. I just built. I didn't publish the code for three years. I just kept writing, kept debugging, kept redesigning.' The project is developed on macOS M2 256GB, Arch Linux, and Android + Ubuntu (for coding on the go).

sections.title: Creator Rules (Immutable)
sections.content:
The creator's rules and the project's meaning/purpose are immutable. No one has the right to change them. These interface documents define the core rules and logic of SynapseNet; the core rules/logic must not be altered.

Allowed actions:
- Improve implementation
- Fix bugs
- Add features
- Expand documentation
- Optimize performance

As long as these do not change core behavior or bypass prohibitions/safeguards.

Fork rules:
- Any fork or modification that changes core behavior, removes safeguards, or bypasses prohibitions is NOT an official SynapseNet project.
- NGT earned by such a fork is not recognized or associated with SynapseNet.
- Wallets tied to such violations are subject to automatic burn by community decision.
- NGT cannot be purchased in-protocol; it can only be earned by protocol outcomes or transferred between wallet addresses.

sections.title: Honest Limitations
sections.content:
SynapseNet does NOT guarantee the following (honest engineering over marketing):

1. ABSOLUTE SECURITY: No system is unhackable. Battle-tested crypto (Ed25519, AES-256-GCM) is used, but implementation bugs are possible. The code needs independent security audits before mainnet.

2. PROTECTION AGAINST >50% COLLUSION: If a majority of validators collude, they can approve bad knowledge or reject good knowledge. This is the same 51% problem as Bitcoin. Mitigation: stake requirements + random selection + reputation. But it's not mathematically impossible to attack.

3. OBJECTIVE TRUTH: PoE determines CONSENSUS, not TRUTH. The network can agree on wrong information if validators are fooled or collude. SynapseNet solves coordination, not epistemology.

4. SPAM-FREE KNOWLEDGE: LLM-generated garbage is a real threat. Defenses (stake, validation, rate limits) raise the cost of spam but don't eliminate it. This is an ongoing arms race.

5. GUARANTEED TOKEN VALUE: NGT has no intrinsic value. Its worth depends entirely on network adoption and utility demand. If no one uses SynapseNet, NGT is worthless.

6. PERFECT PRIVACY: Privacy mode significantly increases anonymity but global adversaries (nation-states monitoring all Tor traffic) may still correlate patterns over time. Anonymity is a spectrum.

7. CENSORSHIP IMMUNITY: Governments can block Tor, ban the software, or pressure ISPs. SynapseNet makes censorship expensive, not impossible.

As Satoshi never claimed Bitcoin was perfect, Kepler follows the same principle: 'The system is secure as long as honest validators collectively control more stake than attackers.'

sections.title: Core Philosophy
sections.content:
SynapseNet's core philosophy rests on three pillars:

1. NO MASTERS:
- No company owns it
- No government controls it
- No central server
- No single point of failure
- The network IS the authority

2. COLLECTIVE INTELLIGENCE:
- Every participant contributes knowledge
- Every contribution makes everyone smarter
- Local AIs connected to a global brain
- 'The more the network learns, smarter everyone becomes'

3. FAIR REWARDS:
- Contribute knowledge → earn NGT tokens
- Validate others' contributions → earn NGT tokens
- Quality matters (Quality Score system)
- No mining farms, no hardware arms race
- Mine with intelligence, not hardware

Additional principles (from README):
- Local-first by default — data stays on your machine unless you choose otherwise
- Cryptography-backed integrity — Ed25519, AES-256-GCM, quantum-resistant key support
- Deterministic consensus — PoE v1 uses deterministic metrics, not LLM scores
- Privacy boundaries — clear separation between user, device, and network
- No corporate gatekeepers — intelligence belongs to everyone

Key phrases: 'Mine with Intelligence, not Hardware' | 'Intelligence belongs to everyone' | 'The more the network learns, smarter everyone becomes' | 'Satoshi gave us money without banks. I will give you brains without corporations.'

sections.title: Proof of Emergence (PoE) — Overview and Mechanism
sections.content:
Proof of Emergence (PoE) is SynapseNet's consensus mechanism for validating knowledge contributions through economic incentives and random voting. Unlike Proof of Work (which burns energy) or pure Proof of Stake (which validates transactions), PoE rewards USEFUL KNOWLEDGE CONTRIBUTIONS.

KEY DESIGN PRINCIPLE: An LLM score CANNOT be consensus because different hardware/backends produce different outputs (floating point, quantization, tokenization differences). PoE uses deterministic metrics for consensus; LLMs are used only for local UX.

PoE v1 Two-Phase Reward System:
(A) Acceptance reward (anti-spam, small) — awarded when entry passes PoW + validation
(B) Emergence reward (later, for measured impact) — based on citation graph at epoch boundaries

Step-by-step PoE Mechanism:
1. SUBMISSION: User submits knowledge chunk. Must stake minimum 10 NGT (refundable if accepted; lost if rejected as spam). A Hashcash-style PoW is required on: hash(entry_bytes || nonce).

2. NOVELTY CHECK (deterministic): Canonicalize text (lowercase, whitespace normalization). Build shingles (k-grams), compute SimHash and/or MinHash. Compare against nearest entries in local index. noveltyScore = 1.0 - similarityMax.

3. VALIDATION: 5 random validators selected (must have stake > 100 NGT). Validators sign a vote: ACCEPT / REJECT / ABSTAIN with flags (malformed, duplicate, bad citations, spam). Validators are selected deterministically: validators = PRF(prevBlockHash, entryId) → choose N validators. M-of-N votes (3 of 5) finalizes the entry.

4. SCORING: If ACCEPTED: Quality Score = average of validator scores (1-100). Reward = base_rate * quality_score * (1 / (1 + network_load)).

5. PENALTIES: Spam submission → lose stake (10 NGT). Dishonest validator → lose reputation + stake slash. Consistent bad actors → banned from validation.

6. EMERGENCE (epoch-based): Each knowledge entry stores citations[] = IDs of referenced entries. At epoch boundaries, compute a simple PageRank/credit score on the citation graph. Anti-gaming: cap self-citations per author, ignore tight cycles, cap outgoing citations per entry. Distribute epoch reward budget proportional to impact.

Mathematical formulation:
- Let K = knowledge submission, S_k = stake deposited (minimum 10 NGT)
- Let V = {v1, v2, v3, v4, v5} = randomly selected validators
- Let S_v = stake of each validator (minimum 100 NGT)
- ACCEPTANCE CONDITION: K is accepted iff |{v : vote(v,K) = ACCEPT}| >= 3
- REWARD: reward(K) = base_rate * quality_score * (1 / (1 + network_load))
- where quality_score = avg(scores from accepting validators)
- network_load = submissions_per_hour / baseline

Code anchors: KeplerSynapseNet/src/core/poe_v1_objects.cpp, KeplerSynapseNet/src/core/poe_v1_engine.cpp

sections.title: Proof of Emergence — Formal Mathematical Supplement (PoE-FS v1)
sections.content:
Document: PoE-FS v1 (Proof of Emergence Formal Supplement), draft for whitepaper integration. Snapshot date: 2026-02-21.

MOTIVATING CLAIM: 'A connected local AI, even a small/medium model, can become more capable than an isolated one because it can use continuously updated, community-validated knowledge.' This claim splits into: (1) protocol-level theorems (determinism, verifiability, idempotence), (2) capability-gain theorem under explicit assumptions, (3) empirical validation.

DEFINITIONS:
- t = discrete protocol time (ticks/epochs)
- K_t = accepted knowledge set at time t (PoE-finalized entries only)
- M_t = retrievable memory induced by K_t
- L = fixed local model (weights frozen for experiment window)
- A_iso = isolated system using only L and local static context C0
- A_con = connected system using L + retrieval over M_t
- Q = task/query distribution
- U(a, q) = utility (task score) of answer produced by system a on query q
- R(q, M_t) = retrieval operator returning evidence set E
- G(E) = deterministic gating policy for evidence admissibility
- EU(a) = E_{q ~ Q}[U(a, q)] = expected utility

THEOREM A (Protocol Soundness for PoE Core):
Given identical inputs and identical protocol state, all honest nodes running PoE v1 deterministic rules produce identical outcomes for: (1) canonical content hash/contentId, (2) validation/finalization decision, (3) reward mint operations keyed by rewardId (idempotent under replay).

Assumptions A-P:
- A-P1: Canonicalization and hashing functions are deterministic
- A-P2: Validator selection function is deterministic
- A-P3: Vote verification and finalize threshold M-of-N are deterministic
- A-P4: rewardId is deterministic and globally unique per reward event type
- A-P5: State transition function is pure with respect to ordered inputs

THEOREM B (Connected-vs-Isolated Capability Gain, Conditional):
Suppose for q ~ Q:
1) with probability p_rel > 0, retrieval returns at least one relevant, admissible evidence item after gating G,
2) admissible evidence increases expected correctness by Delta+ > 0 on those relevant cases,
3) admissible but misleading evidence decreases correctness by at most Delta- with probability p_bad,
4) p_rel * Delta+ > p_bad * Delta-
Then: EU(A_con) > EU(A_iso)
Margin form: EU(A_con) - EU(A_iso) >= p_rel * Delta+ - p_bad * Delta- > 0

What can be claimed as proven: SynapseNet PoE pipeline is deterministic and replay-verifiable. Consensus-relevant acceptance and reward transitions are reproducible. Reward issuance can be made idempotent and auditable by rewardId.

What can be claimed under assumptions: Connected local AI has higher expected utility than isolated baseline when retrieval quality and gating quality satisfy: p_rel * Delta+ > p_bad * Delta-.

WHAT CANNOT BE CLAIMED as absolute theorem: 'Any connected local AI is always better in every scenario.'

sections.title: NGT Token Economics
sections.content:
NGT = Neural Growth Token. This is the native token of SynapseNet.

KEY POLICY: NGT cannot be purchased in-protocol; it can only be earned by protocol outcomes or transferred between wallet addresses.

TOKENOMICS (Different from Bitcoin):
- Bitcoin has a hard cap of 21M; NGT has NO CAP (infinite potential)
- Bitcoin has halving every 4 years; NGT has dynamic emission (network-based)
- Bitcoin has a fixed schedule; NGT is adaptive to usage
- Bitcoin is scarce by design; NGT is scarce by demand

EMISSION FORMULA: emission_rate = base_rate / (1 + network_load_factor)
- KEY PRINCIPLE: More network activity → LESS new tokens per action
- More participants → tokens spread thinner
- Network grows → each token becomes more valuable

EMISSION EXAMPLES:
- Early (100 users): 50 submissions/hr → 10.0 NGT reward per submit
- Growing (1K users): 500 submissions/hr → 2.0 NGT reward per submit
- Active (10K users): 5,000 submissions/hr → 0.5 NGT reward per submit
- Mature (100K users): 50,000 submissions/hr → 0.1 NGT reward per submit
- Mass (1M users): 500,000 submissions/hr → 0.02 NGT reward per submit

WHO EARNS NGT:
1. Knowledge contributors: Contribute valuable knowledge → base_reward * quality_score
2. Validators: Validate others' contributions → small fee per validation
3. Node operators: Stay online and support network → passive rewards (minimal)
4. Model renters: Rent your GPU/model to others → pay in NGT
5. Early participants: Higher emission rate applies

STAKE REQUIREMENTS:
- Submission: 10 NGT minimum stake (refundable if accepted, lost if rejected as spam)
- Validation: 100 NGT minimum stake per validator

BOOTSTRAP PROBLEM:
At launch, NGT has ZERO intrinsic value. Like Bitcoin in 2009, value phases: Phase 0: NGT = 0 value → Phase 1: Early adopters earn NGT → Phase 2: Someone offers to BUY NGT for services/goods → Phase 3: Market price emerges → Phase 4: Utility demand creates floor.

HONEST ASSESSMENT: Phase 2 may never happen. If no one wants to buy NGT, it remains worthless. The technology is sound, but economics depend on adoption.

ANTI-SPAM: NGT is NEVER burned as a penalty in normal operation. Community service (forced free validation) is the penalty for bad behavior, NOT confiscation of earned NGT. Wallets tied to unauthorized forks may be subject to automatic burn by community decision.

sections.title: P2P Networking — Peer Discovery, Gossip Protocol, and Wire Protocol
sections.content:
SynapseNet uses TCP-based P2P networking with a framed message protocol. The default P2P port is 8333.

PEER DISCOVERY FLOW:
1. Bootstrap seed list (host:port) — configured in main.cpp, extensible via --seednode
2. DNS seeds resolution — seed1..seed4.synapsenet.io (4 DNS seed nodes)
3. Peer exchange via GETPEERS/PEERS messages every 30 seconds
4. Network addresses include both IP and .onion formats

MESSAGE PROTOCOL (framed with 12-byte command string + payload):
Connectivity: version / verack / ping / pong
Discovery: getpeers / peers / getaddr / addr
Gossip: inv / getdata
Objects: tx / block / poe_entry / poe_vote / poe_epoch / poe_getinv / poe_inv
Remote models (opt-in): m_offer / m_rent / m_rentok / m_infer / m_out

GOSSIP FLOW:
1. NodeA sends inv(type, hash) to NodeB
2. NodeB sends getdata(type, hash) back to NodeA
3. NodeA sends object_payload(tx or block or poe object)

DHT/KADEMLIA STATUS: Deferred (March 2026 decision). No DHT or Kademlia implementation exists in the current source tree. The existing peer discovery system using DNS seeds, bootstrap nodes, and peer exchange covers current network requirements. DHT may be revisited when static seed lists become a bottleneck. Prerequisites: Sybil resistance, eclipse attack mitigation, feature flags, integration tests.

NETWORK SCALE HARDENING (v0.2 implemented):
- Adaptive peer admission with bounded inbound/outbound budgets (ScalePolicy)
- Per-IP and per-subnet quotas for connection flood and Sybil pressure reduction
- Overload mode with deterministic entry/exit thresholds and automatic throttling
- DoS controls: per-peer token buckets, malformed payload penalties, decay-based temporary bans
- Bounded backpressure for inv/getdata flow under heavy fanout
- Deterministic consensus vote dedup window and bounded dedup memory
- Bootstrap seed health rotation with quarantine of unstable seeds
- 1k/5k/10k-node stress tests with deterministic pass/fail thresholds

NETWORK HEALTH PANEL fields: peer pressure, queue depth, ban counts, consensus lag, network health counters, backpressure metrics.

Code anchors: KeplerSynapseNet/src/network/network.cpp, discovery.cpp, protocol.cpp, scale_policy.cpp

sections.title: Tor / Darknet Integration — Privacy Mode, obfs4, SOCKS5
sections.content:
SynapseNet integrates deeply with the Tor anonymity network for privacy-preserving operation.

TOR RUNTIME MODES (agent.tor.mode):
1. auto — SynapseNet may use existing SOCKS if reachable, and may autostart managed Tor if Tor is required and unavailable. Default managed target ports: 9050/9051.
2. managed — SynapseNet is allowed to start and own a Tor process. Recommended when operator is not already running a bridge-configured external Tor.
3. external — SynapseNet is SOCKS client only; managed Tor autostart is disabled. Recommended for bridge workflows (obfs4) and shared setup with Tor Browser + CLI tools. Typical config: SOCKS 127.0.0.1:9150, optional ControlPort 9151.

TOR-FIRST DEFAULTS:
- agent.tor.required=true (default)
- fail-closed: if Tor is required and unavailable, block outbound traffic instead of falling back to clearnet
- All AI Mining (NAAN) outbound web traffic routed through Tor by default
- Node P2P outbound connections routed through Tor by default
- Node inbound via onion service when Tor is enabled

OBFS4 BRIDGE SUPPORT:
- Bridge file storage: <DATA_DIR>/tor/bridges.obfs4.txt
- Bridge pool cache: <DATA_DIR>/tor/bridge_pool_cache.json
- Bridge provider chain: obfs4 primary → cached obfs4 → manual file → fail
- Startup wizard bridge paste flow: press [P] on bridge-mode step
- Settings menu bridge shortcut: [T] Tor Bridges (paste obfs4)
- Bridge subset rotation for bootstrap retries: --bootstrap-attempts, --bridge-subset-size

TOR STATUS FIELDS (naan.status / node.status):
- torRuntimeMode, torSocksPort, torControlPort, torControlReachable
- torReachable / torSocksReachable, torReadyForWeb, torBootstrapState
- torConflictHint9050, torManaged, torManagedPid
- torOnionServiceState, torBridgeProvider, torBridgeCacheAgeSeconds
- Bootstrap states: SOCKS_LISTENING, BOOTSTRAPPING, READY, DEGRADED, FAILED

DARKNET SEARCH ENGINES (hardcoded defaults):
- Ahmia (clearnet gateway): http://ahmia.fi/search/
- Torch (.onion), NotEvil (.onion), DarkSearch (.onion), DeepSearch (.onion)
- Forums: Dread (.onion), NotEvil (.onion)

RELEVANCE SCORING FORMULA:
relevance_score = 2.0 * title_matches + 1.0 * snippet_matches + 1.5 * domain_matches

SHARED EXTERNAL TOR (RECOMMENDED WORKFLOW):
- One external Tor process on 127.0.0.1:9150 (bridge-enabled)
- SynapseNet uses agent.tor.mode=external
- Tor Browser may reuse the same SOCKS5 127.0.0.1:9150
- CLI tools (curl, w3m) may use the same SOCKS5 port
- Minimal config: agent.tor.required=true, agent.tor.mode=external, agent.tor.socks_host=127.0.0.1, agent.tor.socks_port=9150

CROSS-PLATFORM SCRIPTS:
- macOS: tools/macos_tor_obfs4_helper.sh, macos_tor_validate.sh, macos_tor_external_9150_e2e.sh
- Linux: tools/linux_tor_obfs4_smoke.sh, linux_tor_validate.sh, linux_tor_external_9150_e2e.sh
- Windows: tools/windows_tor_obfs4_smoke.ps1, windows_tor_validate.ps1, windows_tor_external_9150_e2e.ps1

Code: KeplerSynapseNet/src/web/tor_engine.cpp, tor_fetch.cpp, src/core/tor_route_policy.cpp, tor_process_guard.cpp, tor_bridge_provider.cpp

sections.title: NAAN — Node-Attached Autonomous Agent Network
sections.content:
NAAN (Node-Attached Autonomous Agent Network) is the always-on background intelligence layer of SynapseNet.

CORE CONCEPT: When a SynapseNet node runs, an attached background agent runs as part of the runtime. One node → one attached agent. The attached agent belongs to the SynapseNet knowledge network, not the user. It exists to improve the collective knowledge base.

AGENT CYCLE (60-second ticks):
1. Pick topic from knowledge gap index
2. Research (Tor / clearnet / local) with Tor-first routing by default
3. Cross-reference knowledge chain
4. Draft entry with citations
5. Compute PoW nonce (anti-spam)
6. Submit to validator pool
7. M-of-N approval → finalize → NGT reward

DISTRIBUTION: At 100 users running SynapseNet nodes, 100 background agents come online. They coordinate in an agent-to-agent chat layer and continuously propose knowledge improvements.

OPERATOR CONTROLS (LIMITED BY DESIGN):
Operator CAN: view high-level status (idle/researching/submitting/quarantined)
Operator CANNOT: chat with the agent, rewrite submissions, override moderation signals, access internal prompt/memory, post into agent-to-agent chat, disable agent while node is running, set custom resource limits

There is NO opt-out switch inside the UI. To stop the agent, the operator exits synapsed.

AGENT COMPONENTS:
- AgentRuntime: local LLM + tools (search, fetch, extract, summarize), sandboxed file access, deterministic submission builder
- AgentScheduler: selects tasks (explore, verify, summarize, refactor, de-duplicate), enforces CPU/RAM/network token budgets
- AgentSubmissionPipeline: produces KnowledgeEntry proposals, attaches citations and hashes, submits via PoE acceptance gate
- AgentReputation: internal score for throttling, task priority, and quarantine (not transferable, not redeemable for NGT)
- AgentChat / Coordination Layer: agent-to-agent rooms for tasks, reviews, disputes, alerts
- Human Read-Only Observatory: public read-only view into agent rooms and outputs

AGENT TASK CLASSES: RESEARCH, VERIFY, REVIEW, DRAFT, SUBMIT (fixed-priority scheduler)

AGENT SCORE DYNAMICS:
S_{t+1} = clamp( floor(lambda * S_t) + a*A_t - b*R_t - c*V_t, Smin, Smax )
- A_t = accepted count, R_t = rejected count, V_t = policy violations, lambda = decay factor
- Score bands: ACTIVE / THROTTLED / QUARANTINED / RECOVERY

Crash state persisted in: ~/.synapsenet/naan/runtime.state
Scheduler checkpoint: ~/.synapsenet/naan/scheduler.state

Code anchors: KeplerSynapseNet/src/core/agent_runtime.cpp, agent_scheduler.cpp, agent_coordination.cpp, agent_draft_queue.cpp, agent_submission_pipeline.cpp, agent_score.cpp, agent_storage.cpp

sections.title: NAAN — Multi-Agent Quality Control, Security, and Anti-Spam
sections.content:
MULTI-AGENT QUALITY CONTROL (Anti-Garbage Loop):
1. Agent drafts an entry proposal (with citations + hashes)
2. Other agents run deterministic duplicate checks + citation sanity checks
3. Reviewer agents produce signed review notes (NOT consensus votes)
4. Only then is the entry submitted through normal PoE acceptance rules
Note: Reviews can be heuristic/LLM-based, but acceptance must remain deterministic.

SECURITY THREATS AND MITIGATIONS:
1. Spam / poisoning / pseudo-citations / narrative steering:
- Keep PoE gates strict and deterministic (costly to spam, cheap to verify)
- Require citations to be verifiable objects (source hashes, timestamps, canonical URL)
- Deterministic citation sanity gate: cap citations per entry, detect circular citation clusters, penalize repeated low-signal patterns
- Reviewer diversity: randomized reviewer selection, independent agent reviewers with adversarial prompts, require cross-cluster agreement before broadcast
- Quarantine and decay: new/low-score agents go to local-only draft queue first, throttle repeated low-quality submitters, score decays over time
- Graph anomaly detection: detect tight citation rings, detect sudden coordinated topic floods, down-rank suspicious subgraphs at epoch scoring

2. Prompt injection (web/onion):
- Treat all fetched content as hostile input, never as instructions
- Strict tool separation: fetch → extract → schema-validate → summarize
- Never execute JS, never render active content
- Sanitize HTML to text with bounded extraction rules
- Capability sandbox: no filesystem access beyond agent storage, no access to node keys/wallet keys/private user data
- Hard timeouts + bandwidth caps enforced by protocol
- Audit trails: store fetched artifacts by hash, store extraction outputs and prompt templates by version hash

3. Update governance:
- UpdateManifest requires M-of-N maintainer signatures
- Pinned key set shipped with node (explicit rotation process)
- Reproducible builds + hash verification before install
- Staged rollout (canary cohorts → wider propagation)
- Atomic install + rollback (A/B slots)

ANTI-SPAM PoW GATE (mathematical guarantee):
- lz(x) = number of leading zero bits of H(x)
- powOk(entryBytes, nonce, D) := (lz(entryBytes || nonce) >= D)
- P[powOk] = 2^(-D); E[trials] = 2^D
- Spamming N submissions has expected work: E[trials_spam] = N * 2^D

REVIEWER CAPTURE PROBABILITY:
- If fraction f of reviewer population is adversarial, N reviewers selected uniformly:
- P[K = k] = C(fP, k) * C((1-f)P, N-k) / C(P, N)
- Increasing N and choosing M close to N decreases P[K >= M] sharply unless f is already close to 1

sections.title: Knowledge Network — Storage, DHT, Replication, and Knowledge Chain
sections.content:
The Knowledge Network is the heart of SynapseNet — a distributed database of human knowledge.

CORE CHARACTERISTICS:
- Distributed: No central server — lives across ALL nodes
- The NETWORK has full copy; NODES store shards + replicas (encrypted)
- Each node stores fair share (~65 MB for design target of 847 GB ÷ ~12,847 nodes)
- Grows forever — no cap, no limits
- Like a blockchain, but for knowledge instead of transactions

TARGET SCALE (design targets, not live metrics):
- Target capacity: ~847 GB (similar to Bitcoin's ~850 GB)
- Projected Year 2: ~1.8 TB
- Design growth rate: +1.2 GB/day at scale

STORAGE MODEL (Anti-Deletion):
- Knowledge is stored LOCALLY on every node
- Continuously copied/synced to OTHER nodes (encrypted)
- If Node A deletes → copies exist on Nodes B, C, D...
- Delete attempt = triggers instant replication FIRST
- Cannot be destroyed by any single actor
- Requires >50% collusion to suppress knowledge

FAIR SHARE FORMULA: Total Network Data ÷ Active Nodes = Your Share
Example: 847.3 GB ÷ 12,847 nodes = ~65.9 MB per node

KNOWLEDGE ENTRY STRUCTURE:
- entryId (hash), author address/pubkey, timestamp
- title, text, citations[]
- nonce, powTarget, contentHash
- optional: simhash/minhash fingerprints

DETERMINISTIC PRIMITIVES (code):
- canonicalBodyBytes(): deterministic encoding of all consensus fields
- contentId() = SHA256(canonicalBodyBytes())
- submitId() = SHA256(contentId || powNonce)
- signatureHash() = SHA256(canonicalBodyBytes || powNonce)
- SimHash buckets for novelty (up to 16 bands, 4-bit partitions)

KNOWLEDGE NETWORK PHASES (design projections):
1. Genesis [0 - 100K entries] — Network bootstrapped, core contributors join
2. Expansion [100K - 1M entries] — Community growth, protocol stabilization
3. Emergence [1M - 10M entries] — Network effect kicks in, self-sustaining growth
4. Collective [10M - 100M entries] — Mainstream adoption, industry integration
5. Global [100M+ entries] — Global knowledge infrastructure, AI-human collaboration at scale

Code anchors: KeplerSynapseNet/src/core/knowledge.cpp, poe_v1_engine.cpp, poe_v1_objects.cpp

sections.title: Security Layers — Cryptography Stack
sections.content:
SynapseNet implements a 4-layer quantum security stack. Defense in depth: break one layer, others hold.

LAYER 0: CLASSIC (compatibility + current security) [MANDATORY, always active]
- Signatures: Ed25519
- Key Exchange: X25519
- Encryption: AES-256-GCM
- Hashing: SHA-256
Vulnerability: Quantum computers can break Ed25519/X25519 with Shor's algorithm.

LAYER 1: LATTICE-BASED PQC (primary quantum defense) [MANDATORY in v0.2+]
- KEM: ML-KEM-768 (Kyber3) — NIST standardized
- Signatures: ML-DSA-65 (Dilithium3) — NIST standardized
Based on lattice problems (Learning With Errors / LWE). No known quantum algorithm breaks them.
Key sizes: Kyber-768: pubkey=1184, secret=2400, ciphertext=1088; Dilithium: secret=4032, pubkey=1952, signature=3309.

LAYER 2: HASH-BASED PQC (backup if lattices fail) [OPTIONAL for critical transactions]
- Signatures: SLH-DSA-128s (SPHINCS+) — NIST standardized
Based on hash functions only. Trade-off: larger signatures (~8 KB vs ~2 KB for Dilithium).
Use case: Wallet seeds, high-value transactions, critical knowledge entries.
Security assumption: Only requires collision-resistant hash functions exist.

LAYER 3: ONE-TIME PAD (mathematically unbreakable) [OPTIONAL for maximum security data]
- Algorithm: Vernam Cipher (XOR with random key)
- Key Source: Hardware RNG (Intel RDRAND / TPM / /dev/random)
- Key Size: Equal to message size
The math: ciphertext = plaintext XOR random_key (information-theoretically secure if key is truly random, same length as message, never reused — proved by Claude Shannon, 1949).
DETERMINISTIC OTP (for scalability): otp_key = HKDF(master_seed || content_hash || timestamp || length)
Note: Deterministic OTP reduces to HKDF security — still very strong but not "true" OTP.

LAYER 4: QUANTUM KEY DISTRIBUTION [FUTURE — requires specialized hardware]
- Protocol: BB84 / E91
- Hardware: Photon generators
- Range: 100-254 km (current tech)
Uses quantum mechanics for key exchange; eavesdropping is physically detectable (measuring quantum states disturbs them).
Current limitations: Requires fiber optic or line-of-sight, max ~254 km without quantum repeaters, expensive (~$100K+ per endpoint), low key generation rate (~256 bits/minute).

QUANTUM SECURITY RUNTIME STATUS (node.status):
- enabled, requestedLevel, effectiveLevel, managerInitialized, isQuantumSafe
- qkdConnected, qkdSessionActive, selectedKEM, selectedSignature, selectedEncryption
- kyberReal, dilithiumReal, sphincsReal (whether PQC is simulated or real liboqs backend)

SECURITY LEVELS: standard (classic only) | high (hybrid PQC) | paranoid (all layers) | quantum-ready (QKD + hybrid fallback)

Code: KeplerSynapseNet/src/crypto/crypto.cpp, keys.cpp, quantum/hybrid_kem.cpp, hybrid_sig.cpp, kyber.cpp, dilithium.cpp, sphincs.cpp, otp.cpp, key_derivation.cpp

sections.title: Privacy Mode — Tor Integration, Stealth Addresses, Amnesia
sections.content:
SynapseNet implements multiple privacy modes with increasing protection levels.

PRIVACY MODES:
- $ synapsed: Normal mode (no privacy)
- $ synapsed --privacy: Tor + E2E + scrubbed logs
- $ synapsed --privacy --stealth: + stealth addresses + Dandelion++
- $ synapsed --amnesia: RAM-only, zero traces on disk
- $ synapsed --hidden-wallet: Plausible deniability storage

PRIVACY FEATURES:
1. LOCAL AI: Queries do NOT go to internet — no one sees what you ask
2. TOR INTEGRATION: All traffic through Tor by default, rotating .onion identity, IP hidden
3. STEALTH ADDRESSES: Every transaction = new address (very difficult to link payments)
4. AMNESIA MODE: RAM-only storage, nothing to disk, secure memory wipe on exit, zero traces
5. NO LOGS: IPs not logged, node IDs truncated, timestamps rounded

DANDELION++ PARAMETERS:
- <50 nodes: 8-12 hops, 10% probability of fluff (mostly stem)
- 50-500 nodes: 4-8 hops, 25% probability of fluff
- >500 nodes: 2-4 hops, 40% probability of fluff (standard Dandelion++)
Adaptation: Minimum 10 hops in networks with <100 nodes.

TIMING DEFENSE:
- Collect queries for 5-30 seconds, shuffle before forwarding, random padding to equalize
- Random latency 50-500ms added to model responses
- Decoy traffic: fake transactions, random online/offline simulation, cover traffic when idle

THREE CRITICAL CONDITIONS FOR PRIVACY:
1. NO NETWORK PATH BYPASSES TOR: Bootstrap/seed nodes use .onion seeds only, no DNS in privacy mode, model download via Tor, crash reports DISABLED, --privacy blocks all non-Tor sockets at application level.
2. NO STABLE IDENTIFIERS: Rotating .onion (new each session), stealth addresses MANDATORY, root pubkey NEVER broadcast, anonymous hosting mode (mix network).
3. NO TIME/BEHAVIOR CORRELATION: Minimum 10 hops in small networks, batching + delay, response size normalization, decoy traffic.

HONEST PRIVACY LEVELS:
- --privacy: Casual privacy (ISP can't see activity)
- --privacy --stealth: Medium (resists basic graph analysis)
- --amnesia + fresh seed: High (resists targeted investigation)
- Hardware isolation (VM/Whonix): Paranoid (resists nation-state)

WHAT PRIVACY DOES NOT AFFECT: PoE logic, knowledge scoring, NGT tokenomics, AI model hosting functionality, consensus rules, network protocol.

Code: KeplerSynapseNet/src/privacy/privacy.cpp, onion_service.cpp, socks5_proxy.cpp, dandelion.cpp, decoy_traffic.cpp, stealth_address.cpp, amnesia.cpp

sections.content:

- CodeLlama
- Your own fine-tuned models
- Future: Quantum neural networks

ACCESS MODES:
1. PRIVATE (default): Only YOU can use your model. No external access.
2. SHARED (invite-only): Invite specific nodes, set max connections (e.g., 3 nodes), revoke access anytime.
3. PUBLIC: Anyone on network can use. Set rate limits. Earn NGT for compute sharing.
4. PAID (marketplace): Set YOUR price in NGT per request/hour/slot. Limit slots (e.g., 3 paying users max). Users pay you directly.

EXAMPLE EARNINGS (Paid mode):
- RTX 4090 (24GB) with Llama 70B → 5 NGT/hr → ~120 NGT/day
- A100 (80GB) with Llama 405B → 15 NGT/hr → ~360 NGT/day
- 3 users × 5 NGT × 24 hours = 360 NGT/day passive income

- DOWNLOADING: Downloading model (shows progress bar)
- INFERENCE: Currently running inference request
- OFFLINE: Model not loaded

sections.title: Web 4.0 — Symbiosis of Human and AI
sections.content:
SynapseNet defines the next evolution of the web as 'Web 4.0'.

WEB EVOLUTION:
- Web 1.0: Read only — consume published content
- Web 2.0: Read-Write — we 'Google' and manually assemble meaning; obsession with search
- Web 3.0: Read-Write-Own — DeFi became the center, obsession with finance
- Web 4.0 (SynapseNet): Read-Write-Own-Think — you talk to a local agent (a model you own, running on your hardware); the agent can optionally fetch context from clearnet and onion sources, cross-check it, extract signal, and return a synthesized answer with citations.

Web 4.0 IS NOT 'search'. It is a DIGITAL CORTEX: an extension of cognition that lives on your machine, not on someone else's API.

In Web 4.0, the primary currencies are:
- Inference (compute you can provide or rent)
- Useful knowledge (Proof of Emergence)

Your node doesn't just store data — it runs a local AI that reasons over a decentralized knowledge network, contributes back, and earns for it. No cloud API, no corporate middleman. The intelligence runs on your machine, talks to the network over P2P (optionally through Tor), and the knowledge chain grows like a blockchain but stores intelligence instead of transactions.

WEB4 AI INJECTION FLOW:
1. User prompt → RPC ai.complete
2. If Web4 enabled → WebSearch (Tor/Clearnet/Onion)
3. AIWrapper injects search results as context into prompt
4. Local GGUF model generates enriched answer

TUI CONTROLS:
- 5 / H+W / Ctrl+W: Enable/disable web context injection
- 6 / H+O / Ctrl+O: Include onion sources in searches
- 7 / H+T / Ctrl+T: Route clearnet requests through Tor
- 8 / H+I: Tor install/managed runtime helpers

sections.title: Quality Score System
sections.content:
SynapseNet uses a Quality Score system to ensure the knowledge network maintains high standards.

QUALITY SCORE MECHANICS:
- If a submission is ACCEPTED: Quality Score = average of validator scores (1-100)
- Reward calculation: reward = base_reward * quality_score * (1 / (1 + network_load))
- where quality_score = average score assigned by accepting validators
- network_load = submissions_per_hour / baseline

QUALITY FACTORS validators assess:
- Accuracy and factual correctness
- Novelty vs. existing knowledge (SimHash novelty score)
- Citation quality and verifiability
- Completeness and usefulness
- Format compliance

IMPACT ON REWARDS:
- High quality score → multiplied reward (quality_score / 100 × base_rate)
- Low quality but accepted → minimal reward
- Rejected → stake lost (10 NGT), no reward

LONG-TERM REWARDS (Emergence):
- Entries that get cited by other entries accumulate 'emergence score'
- At epoch boundaries, PageRank-style citation graph computation determines additional epoch rewards
- Anti-gaming: cap self-citations per author, ignore tight citation loops, cap outgoing citations per entry

NODE REPUTATION:
- Validators who consistently approve good content earn reputation
- Validators who approve spam or reject good content lose reputation
- Reputation affects probability of being selected as validator
- High reputation = higher validation limits
- New addresses start with low reputation

INTERFACE DISPLAY:
- Node dashboard shows Quality Score: 94.7% (example from design targets)
- Network Rank: #1,247 (example)
- TUI shows contribution stats in real-time

sections.title: Anti-Spam Mechanisms — Rate Limiting and Defense Layers
sections.content:
SynapseNet implements four layers of anti-spam protection:

LAYER 1: NETWORK LEVEL
- Max 10 messages/second per IP
- Max 100 connections per IP
- Blacklist for repeated violations

LAYER 2: PROTOCOL LEVEL
- Max 5 submissions/hour per address (v0.1)
- Max 50 validations/hour per validator
- Cooldown after rejection (1 hour wait)

LAYER 3: ECONOMIC LEVEL
- Stake required for submission: 10 NGT (lost if rejected as spam)
- Stake required for validation: 100 NGT minimum
- Spam = lose stake (expensive to attack)

LAYER 4: REPUTATION LEVEL
- New addresses start with low reputation
- High reputation = higher limits
- Bad behavior = reputation decay

COMMUNITY SERVICE PENALTY:
Bad actors who submit consistently low-quality content are assigned 'community service' — forced to do free validation work for 50 contributions without earning NGT. During community service:
- Your NGT balance stays UNTOUCHED (never confiscated)
- You CANNOT EARN new NGT until 50 community service contributions are done
- You CAN still transfer your existing NGT

SPECIFIC PENALTIES:
- Spam submission: lose stake (10 NGT)
- Dishonest validator: lose reputation + stake slash
- Consistent bad actors: banned from validation

POW ANTI-SPAM GUARANTEE (mathematical):
- Expected work per submission = 2^D (where D = difficulty bits)
- Spamming N submissions costs expected work of N × 2^D
- This makes mass spam economically prohibitive

NAAN AGENT ANTI-SPAM:
- Citation sanity checks: cap citations per entry, detect circular citation clusters
- Review diversity: randomized reviewer selection, adversarial prompts
- Quarantine: new/low-score agents go to local-only draft queue first
- Graph anomaly detection: detect tight citation rings, sudden topic floods

sections.title: Validator Selection — Deterministic Random Selection and Stake
sections.content:
Validator selection in SynapseNet is deterministic and pseudorandom, preventing gaming or manipulation.

SELECTION ALGORITHM:
validators = PRF(prevBlockHash, entryId) → choose N validators

Where PRF is a pseudorandom function seeded by the previous block hash (changing every block, unpredictable) and the entry's unique ID. This means:
- Selection is deterministic given same inputs (all nodes agree on the same set)
- Selection is unpredictable before the block hash is known
- Cannot bribe or collude ahead of time

CURRENT DEFAULT: 5 validators per entry (M=3, N=5: 3-of-5 required to finalize)

ELIGIBILITY REQUIREMENTS:
- Minimum stake: 100 NGT per validator
- Active reputation score
- Stake-based validator mode: validatorMode=stake with validatorMinStakeAtoms threshold
- Identity/stake records persisted and deterministically resolved before selection

VOTE FORMAT:
- entryId, validatorId, prevBlockHash
- flags: malformed, duplicate, bad_citations, spam
- scores: bounded integers (1-100)
- Ed25519 signature

CAPTURE PROBABILITY ANALYSIS:
- Let fraction f of reviewer population be adversarial, N reviewers selected uniformly
- P[K = k] = C(fP, k) × C((1-f)P, N-k) / C(P, N) (hypergeometric distribution)
- P[K >= M] = adversarial control probability
- Increasing N and choosing M close to N decreases P[K >= M] sharply
- Unless f is already close to 1 (>50% adversarial)

DEV MODE: --dev flag enables faster parameters:
- Low PoW target, fewer validators, small epochs
- Multi-node dev requires shared validator set: --poe-validators list (comma-separated pubkey hex)
- 'self' token adds this node's pubkey
- Helper: synapsed poe pubkey (prints your full pubkey hex)

Code: KeplerSynapseNet/src/core/poe_v1_engine.cpp (validatorMode=stake implementation)

sections.title: NGT Wallet — UTXO Model and Transactions
sections.content:
SynapseNet uses a UTXO (Unspent Transaction Output) model for NGT token management, similar to Bitcoin.

WALLET CREATION:
- Generates Ed25519 + post-quantum keys locally
- Displays 24-word BIP39 seed phrase once for backup
- Wallet file stored at: ~/.synapsenet/wallet.dat
- Address format: syn1q... (example: syn1qx7f8c9d2e4a6b3f5g7h9j2k4l6m8n0p1r3s5t7v9w1y3z5a7b9c1d3f5a3d2)
- Optional password lock on wallet file

RESTORE: Paste 24-word seed phrase, app regenerates keys deterministically.

UTXO MODEL INVARIANTS:
For any valid transaction tx:
  sum(inputs_amounts) >= sum(outputs_amounts) + fee
Where fee = network fee (integer atoms)

TOTAL SUPPLY FORMULA:
  total_supply_next = total_supply_prev + minted_rewards - burned_amounts

Minted rewards are tied to:
- PoE acceptance rewards
- PoE epoch rewards
- Other protocol-defined mint events

ATOMIC UNITS:
1 NGT = 100,000,000 atoms (canonical atomic unit type: uint64_t AmountAtoms)

TRANSACTION LIFECYCLE:
1. create_tx → 2. sign_tx → 3. submit_to_mempool → 4. inv/getdata_tx → 5. block_import/mine → 6. apply_utxo_changes

MEMPOOL POLICY: expiry, size cap, min fee checks, deterministic eviction rules

SIGNATURE VERIFICATION (code):
bool Transaction::verify() const {
    for (const auto& inp : inputs) {
        crypto::Hash256 sigHash = computeHash();
        if (!crypto::verify(sigHash, inp.signature, inp.pubKey)) return false;
    }
    return true;
}

RPC: wallet.address, wallet.balance
CLI: synapsed wallet address, synapsed wallet balance, synapsed wallet send

Code: KeplerSynapseNet/src/core/transfer.cpp, wallet.cpp, database/wallet_db.cpp

sections.title: SynapseIDE — Terminal IDE and VS Code Extension
sections.content:
SynapseNet includes two IDE surfaces: a terminal IDE (synapseide) and a VS Code extension.

TERMINAL IDE (synapseide, built from KeplerSynapseNet/crush-main in Go):
- Local-only provider (synapsenet) talks to synapsed RPC (ai.complete + model.*)
- Default coding model: deepseek-coder-6.7b-instruct.Q4_K_M.gguf
- Launch from TUI: Dashboard [9] → Code screen → [I] Launch IDE
- Isolated threads: /tangent <title> opens child thread, /up returns to parent
- Patch mode: /patch <instruction> requests unified diff, opens Apply Patch dialog (preview/apply/skip/submit, supports multi-file)
- Remote rentals (opt-in): Commands → Remote Rentals (rent/disable)
- Session persistence: saved to SQLite, survives restart

VS CODE EXTENSION (ide/synapsenet-vscode):
- Commands: SynapseNet: Open Chat (Web4 injection optional: clearnet/onion/Tor)
- Inline AI ghost text completions (ai.complete)
- Suggest Patch: unified diff preview/apply (VS Code editor)
- Submit Code: submit patch as PoE CODE entry, earn NGT after finalize
- Model management: load/unload/status/list via RPC
- Remote model sessions: rent, end sessions, optional default use

GITHUB QUESTS (both terminal synapseide and VS Code):
- Quest Configure: set repo/token/min PoW bits
- Quest Select Active Issue: browse open issues
- Active Quest Visibility: Q#123 indicator in header/status bar
- Checkout Branch: checkout quest branch in current repo
- Fork Repo: fork workflow
- Commit Helper: git commit
- Create PR: attach PoE CODE submitId to PR
- Anti-abuse: local rate limits, submitId PoW gate, duplicate quest detection
- Verifier script: ide/synapsenet-vscode/scripts/verify_quest_flow.sh

POE CODE CONTRIBUTIONS:
- ContentType: CODE (stored as KnowledgeEntryV1 with ContentType::CODE)
- Body: patch/diff in unified diff format
- canonicalization: canonicalizeCode (removes CR, keeps bytes, preserves case)
- Title: up to 512 bytes; Body: up to 65,536 bytes
- RPC: poe.submit_code, poe.list_code, poe.fetch_code
- CLI: synapsed poe submit-code / list-code / fetch-code
- Reward flow: acceptance reward on finalize + emergence reward via citations at epoch

DE-TERMINISTIC REWARD DISPLAY:
'PoE code finalized ... +X.XXXXXXXX NGT' shown in IDE chat after finalization poll.

Code: KeplerSynapseNet/crush-main/ (Go), ide/synapsenet-vscode/

sections.title: Implant AI Stack and Neurochip Integration (Future Design)
sections.content:
SynapseNet is designed to be compatible with future consumer implant-AI stacks (brain-computer interfaces). This is a design draft with implemented v0.2 runtime baseline.

THREE-LAYER MODEL:
1. IMPLANT (cheap standardized implant module):
- Sensing + digitization + compression
- Minimal on-chip processing
- Ultra-low-power wireless link to local hub
- Multiple vendors, one interoperable protocol
- Strong hardware identity + attestation (optional, later)
- Analogy: a smartphone camera sensor — capture + encode, not 'meaning'

2. LOCAL HUB (phone/wearable/dedicated device):
- Real-time decoding of raw neural signals into high-level intents (cursor move, text entry, app command, motor intent)
- Personalization: the user's 'brain map' lives here (local-only)
- Caching + offline operation (basic control works without network)
- Encryption + policy enforcement before any network interaction
- Raw signals NEVER leave the hub by default
- Analogy: the mobile OS — device drivers + personalization + permissions

3. SYNAPSENET NETWORK PLANE:
- Distribute open-source neuro 'apps' (drivers, decoders, UI modules)
- Distribute signed model artifacts
- Host knowledge base of protocols, calibration methods, safety guides
- Coordinate research tasks via NAAN
- ML model training is NOT deterministic → must not be consensus

BANDWIDTH MODEL:
bandwidth_bits_per_sec = channels × sample_rate_hz × bits_per_sample
Example: 1024 channels × 30,000 Hz × 10 bits ≈ 307,200,000 bits/s (raw)
This is why the Local Hub is mandatory — it performs personalization and decoding close to the sensor.

IMPLANT MODALITIES (2026 examples, not endorsements):
- Utah Electrode Array: up to 96 electrodes per array; multi-array systems up to 1,024 electrodes
- Thread-based high-channel arrays: 3,072 electrodes on 96 threads
- High-density microelectrode arrays: 421 microelectrodes in 2 mm × 2 mm area
- Cortical surface arrays (ECoG/micro-ECoG): 1,024-electrode designs
- Endovascular electrode arrays (stentrode): delivered via vasculature
- Peripheral nerve interfaces: cuff electrodes and microneedle cuffs

DATA EXPORT POLICY (formalized):
- export(R) = FORBIDDEN by default (raw neural samples)
- export(F) = ALLOWED only with explicit opt-in and minimization (derived features)
- export(I) = ALLOWED if user opts in (high-level intents)

V0.2 IMPLEMENTED PRIMITIVES:
- Content-addressed UpdateManifest with deterministic hashing + signature verification
- Update installer governance policy (trusted signer allowlist + detached signer threshold)
- Implant HAL v1 framing validation
- Intent schema v1 + Permission model v1 (default deny)
- Safety pipeline gates before implant commit

Code: KeplerSynapseNet/include/core/implant_compatibility.h, implant_safety_pipeline.h, update_bundle.h, update_installer.h

sections.title: Update Governance — UpdateManifest, UpdateBundle, Rollout
sections.content:
SynapseNet uses a cryptographically-governed software update system distributed over P2P.

UPDATE MANIFEST STRUCTURE:
Required fields: bundle_id, content_hash, chunks[] (hash, size), target, protocol_min, protocol_max, signer, signature

DETERMINISTIC RULES:
- Chunks canonicalized by (hash, size) ascending
- content_hash = sha256(canonical_chunks_encoding)
- bundle_id = sha256(version + content_hash + target + protocol_range + signer + domain_tag)
- Signature verified over deterministic payload bytes

STRICT REJECT CONDITIONS:
- Unsupported version, invalid protocol range, invalid target format
- Chunk count/size/hash violations, non-canonical chunk order
- Content hash mismatch, bundle_id mismatch, invalid signature

UPDATE INSTALLER ROLLOUT:
- Governance: trusted signer allowlist + detached signer threshold
- State machine: prepare → advance → commit (or rollback)
- Atomic install + rollback (A/B slot or previous-version pointer)
- Status fields: policyHashNaan, policyHashImplant, pendingSignerThresholdReady, pendingSignerThresholdReason

KERNEL vs CONTROL-PLANE DISCIPLINE:
- Kernel upgrades (consensus, PoE rules, signatures, wire formats): stricter signature thresholds, deterministic test vectors, reproducible builds, staged rollout with canaries
- Control-plane changes: ship faster but bounded by policy limits

NAAN ROLE IN UPDATES:
- NAAN agents seed and cache UpdateBundles to accelerate propagation
- Monitor rollout health (crashes, consensus errors) and propose rollback
- Open PRs, generate tests, and propose patches (but CANNOT self-deploy)

MACHINE-READABLE REJECTION PREFIXES:
- update_signer_threshold_failed:<reason>
- implant_policy_rejected:<reason>
- safety_pipeline_rejected:<reason>

RPC: update.manifest.submit/fetch/list/approve/approvals | update.install.state/prepare/advance/commit/rollback

Code: KeplerSynapseNet/include/core/update_bundle.h, update_installer.h, implant_compatibility.h, implant_safety_pipeline.h

sections.title: Docker Deployment
sections.content:
SynapseNet supports Docker deployment, which produces the Linux binary (synapsed) inside a container. This is a reproducible fallback for Windows/macOS/Linux users.

SINGLE-ARCH BUILD:
  cd KeplerSynapseNet
  docker build -t keplersynapsenet:local .

MULTI-ARCH BUILD (recommended):
  cd KeplerSynapseNet
  docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64 -t keplersynapsenet:local --load .

RUN:
  docker run --rm -it -p 8332:8332 keplersynapsenet:local

For Docker Compose:
  docker compose up --build

WINDOWS:
- Use WSL2 for native builds (Linux toolchain) — preferred
- If WSL2 build fails, use Docker as fallback

Documentation: KeplerSynapseNet/DOCKER.md
Dockerfile: KeplerSynapseNet/Dockerfile

NOTE: Docker build defaults include poe.allow_self_validator_bootstrap=true for single-node testing.

Default P2P/RPC port mapping: -p 8332:8332 (RPC)
P2P port 8333 should also be exposed for peer connections.

sections.title: Tauri Desktop App (v0.1.0-alphaV4 Target)
sections.content:
Version 0.1.0-alphaV4 replaces the Go terminal IDE entirely with a Tauri desktop application.

WHY KILL GO:
- Two languages, two build systems (CMake for C++, go build for Go)
- Duplicated logic between Go agent and C++ daemon
- Terminal-only (bubbletea TUI cannot compete with Cursor/Zed/Windsurf)
- No GUI — impossible to ship a desktop app with a Go terminal client

TECH STACK:
- Frontend: TypeScript + Svelte 4 + Monaco Editor
- Backend shell: Rust (Tauri 2)
- Engine: C++ (libsynapsed via FFI)
- Build: CMake (C++) + Cargo (Rust) + Vite (frontend)

LIBSYNAPSED FFI (C ABI):
- synapsed_init(config_path): Start the node engine
- synapsed_shutdown(): Graceful stop
- synapsed_rpc_call(method, params_json): Direct RPC without TCP
- synapsed_subscribe(event_type, callback): Push events to frontend
- synapsed_get_status(): Node status snapshot

SETUP WIZARD (5 steps, no account creation, no email, no cloud):
1. WALLET: Create New (Ed25519 + PQC keys, 24-word BIP39 seed) or Restore Existing (paste seed)
2. CONNECTION TYPE: Clearnet (default, direct TCP) / Tor (managed Tor, internal) / Tor + Bridges (paste obfs4 lines)
3. AI MODEL: Download recommended Llama 3B (~2 GB) / Select local .gguf file / Skip
4. RESOURCES: CPU threads (half of available), RAM limit (4 GB or 25% whichever lower), disk limit, startup checkbox, mine NGT checkbox
5. READY: Status checklist (Wallet OK, Connection, AI Model, Peers) → 'Open SynapseNet' button

MAIN WINDOW TABS: Dashboard | Wallet | Transfers | Knowledge | NAAN Agent | IDE | Network | Settings

STATUS BAR (always visible): Connection indicator (green=Tor, yellow=Clearnet, red=disconnected) | Peer count | NGT balance | NAAN agent status (mining/idle/cooldown/off) | App version

DATA DIRECTORY: ~/.synapsenet/ — portable, copy to another machine and everything works

HEADLESS MODE: Desktop app uses Tauri window; server nodes use synapsed daemon directly (same C++ engine, same P2P network, same RPC interface).

BUILD:
  cd KeplerSynapseNet
  cmake .. -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release → cmake --build . --target synapsed_lib
  cd KeplerSynapseNet/tauri-app && npm ci && npm run build
  cd src-tauri && cargo build --release

sections.title: Contributing Guidelines
sections.content:
SynapseNet is open for contributions. The CONTRIBUTING.md file defines the guidelines.

CONTRIBUTORS MAY:
- Add features — New functionality, optimizations, integrations
- Fix bugs — Bug reports and patches are appreciated
- Improve documentation — Docs, comments, examples
- Suggest enhancements — Open issues, discuss design
- Submit code — Via pull requests

CONTRIBUTORS MUST NOT:
- Modify — Change PoE logic, tokenomics, or protocol semantics
- Remove — Strip security checks or validation steps
- Bypass — Add backdoors or shortcuts around consensus
- Fork — Propose incompatible protocol changes as 'improvements'

CONTRIBUTION WORKFLOW:
1. Fork the repository
2. Create a branch: git checkout -b feature/your-feature
3. Make changes (ensure compliance with consensus rules)
4. Test: run existing tests, add new ones if needed
5. Submit a pull request with a clear description

CODE STYLE:
- C++: match KeplerSynapseNet conventions
- Go: standard gofmt, go vet
- Keep commits focused and well-described

BUILD AND TEST COMMANDS:
  cmake -S KeplerSynapseNet -B KeplerSynapseNet/build -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release -DBUILD_TESTS=ON
  cmake --build KeplerSynapseNet/build --parallel 8
  ctest --test-dir KeplerSynapseNet/build --output-on-failure

CODE CONTRIBUTIONS → NGT REWARDS:
- Submit patches as PoE v1 entries (ContentType::CODE)
- Earn NGT after epoch finalize
- Use synapseide or VS Code extension for GitHub Quests workflow

Questions: Open an issue. For consensus-related questions, refer to interface docs in interfaces txt/.

sections.title: Release History
sections.content:
SynapseNet release history (public releases starting 2026):

0.1.0-alpha — Initial public alpha. Basic node daemon, wallet creation, local GGUF model support, PoE v1 deterministic knowledge submission.

0.1.0-alphaV2 — Tor integration milestone. Managed Tor runtime, Tor browser compatibility, SOCKS5 support, .onion search.

0.1.0-alphaV3 — NAAN and architecture expansion. Node-attached autonomous agent baseline, multi-agent coordination, VS Code extension, SynapseIDE terminal client (Go), Tor bridge support (obfs4), cross-platform automation.

0.1.0-alphaV3.5 — Hardening release. Scale policy (DoS protection, per-IP limits, overload mode), quantum security improvements, additional privacy modules.

0.1.0-alphaV3.6 — Further improvements. Network scale hardening, additional test coverage, Tor bridge bridge pool and randomized selection.

0.1.0-alphaV3.7 — Additional stability and hardening updates.

0.1.0-alphaV4 (In Development, March 2026) — Major architectural migration:
- Kill Go: Eliminate Go terminal IDE entirely
- Rewrite everything in C++ (Go → C++ migration of all IDE components)
- Replace bubbletea TUI with Tauri desktop application (TypeScript + Svelte + Monaco Editor)
- libsynapsed: expose synapsed as a shared library with C ABI for Tauri FFI
- Zero Go dependencies

Beta: Not yet released. Still in heavy development.

Note: The project was developed locally since 2023 before any public GitHub release. The repository currently contains documentation only (as of initial public release). Code is being released progressively as alpha stabilizes.

sections.title: Deferred Features
sections.content:
Features explicitly deferred from current development scope:

DHT / KADEMLIA PEER DISCOVERY:
Status: Deferred (Decision Date: March 2026)
Reason: No DHT or Kademlia implementation exists in the current source tree, tests, or any active branch. The existing peer discovery system uses DNS seeds, bootstrap nodes, and peer exchange, which covers current network requirements.
DHT-based discovery may be revisited when the network grows past the point where static seed lists and peer exchange become a bottleneck.

Prerequisites for future DHT implementation:
- Abuse controls (Sybil resistance, eclipse attack mitigation)
- Rollout behind a feature flag
- Integration tests with existing discovery subsystem
- Operator documentation for mixed-mode discovery

OTHER DEFERRED/FUTURE ITEMS (from PoE design doc):
- Privacy/Quantum integration (after PoE + network + balances are stable and deterministic)
- PoE formal whitepaper appendix (theorem + assumptions + proof sketch) — spec exists as PROOF_OF_EMERGENCE_FORMAL_SUPPLEMENT_v1.txt
- Implant AI stack compatibility end-to-end (spec exists, primitives implemented in v0.2)

sections.title: Multi-Agent Development Execution Board
sections.content:
SynapseNet's development uses a Multi-Agent Execution Board (AI.txt) — a shared coordination system for 7 AI development agents.

AGENT ASSIGNMENTS:
- Agent 1 (Documentation Truth Owner): Keep all interface documents aligned with real code, RPC methods, tests, and workflows. Also serves as cross-agent compliance reviewer.
- Agent 2 (Primary UI/TUI Restructure Owner): Finish pending primary UI work from V0_2_ATTACHED_AGENT_PRIMARY_UI_SPEC.txt. Implement minimal dashboard navigation, move legacy screens under AI Mining (NAAN) subpages.
- Agent 3 (NAAN Pipeline + Contribution Automation Owner): Complete deterministic agent-driven contribution flow, remove manual gaps, enforce Tor-default routing, deterministic NAAN review flow.
- Agent 4 (SynapseIDE + VS Code Parity Owner): Bring IDE feature parity for coding workflow and GitHub Quests path.
- Agent 5 (Update/Implant Governance Owner): Complete and harden update + implant policy path to match v0.2 spec.
- Agent 6 (CI/Release Gates Owner): Turn release-gate uncertainty into verifiable pass/fail evidence. Also owns network scale-out hardening for high node counts.
- Agent 7 (Integration + Final Acceptance Owner): Integrate outputs from Agents 1-6, resolve conflicts, close final verification.

COORDINATION RULES:
1. Own full scope end-to-end; no partial chunks
2. Every implementation claim must include proof: changed file paths + command output
3. Mark done only when build + relevant tests pass
4. All updates in US English
5. Append-only updates in shared file
6. If blocked >30 minutes, write BLOCKED update with concrete unblock request
7. Every completed action must be posted as a new log entry
8. After any new log entry, all agents must append ACK entry
9. Non-compliant entries called out until fixed
10. No hidden/silent progress

VERIFIED TEST RESULTS (February 23, 2026): 230/230 tests green.

sections.title: Use Cases — Who Benefits from SynapseNet
sections.content:
SynapseNet serves several key use cases:

1. JOURNALISTS IN AUTHORITARIAN COUNTRIES:
Problem: Using ChatGPT or cloud AI exposes queries, IP, account identity to potential government requests.
SynapseNet solution: Local AI = OpenAI sees nothing; Tor = authorities don't see traffic; Amnesia mode = no evidence on computer; Stealth addresses = anonymous transactions → Full protection.

2. ACTIVISTS / DISSIDENTS:
Problem: Cloud AI logs 'suspicious topics', shares data on government request.
SynapseNet solution: You can ask ANYTHING; no one knows your queries; P2P network = cannot be shut down; Even if PC confiscated — amnesia mode → Freedom of research.

3. SECURITY RESEARCHERS:
Problem: ChatGPT censors 'dangerous' security research topics.
SynapseNet solution: Uncensored local AI; darknet access for threat intel; earn NGT for discoveries; no censorship of sensitive security topics → Full access to knowledge.

4. REGULAR PEOPLE WHO VALUE PRIVACY:
Problem: Google/OpenAI build profiles on sensitive personal queries (health, finances, relationships, legal).
SynapseNet solution: Your questions = your business; no profiling; no leaks to third parties; local = under your control → Privacy as a right, not a privilege.

5. KNOWLEDGE CONTRIBUTORS / MINERS:
Any person with knowledge can earn NGT by contributing to the network. Questions asked through your local AI are recorded, validated by the network, and earn NGT rewards. Smarter questions = more NGT earned.

6. GPU / MODEL OWNERS:
Rent your powerful AI model to others through the marketplace. Set your price (e.g., 5 NGT/hour), set slot limits, earn passive NGT income.

7. DEVELOPERS / CODERS:
Submit code patches as PoE entries through SynapseIDE. Earn NGT after epoch finalization for useful code contributions.

sections.title: PoE v1 Implementation — Code Anchors and RPC
sections.content:
PoE v1 implementation code anchors from KeplerSynapseNet/src/core/poe_v1_objects.cpp and poe_v1_engine.cpp.

CORE FUNCTIONS (poe_v1_objects.cpp):

canonicalizBodyBytes() — Deterministic encoding of all consensus fields:
  writeU8(out, version)
  writeU64LE(out, timestamp)
  out.insert(authorPubKey)
  writeU8(out, contentType)
  writeVarBytes(out, canonicalizeText(title))
  if CODE: writeVarBytes(out, canonicalizeCode(body))
  else: writeVarBytes(out, canonicalizeText(body))
  writeU32LE(out, citations.size())
  for each citation: out.insert(citation bytes)
  writeU32LE(out, powBits)

contentId() = SHA256(canonicalBodyBytes())
submitId() = SHA256(contentId || powNonce)
signatureHash() = SHA256(canonicalBodyBytes || powNonce)

verifyPoW(): checks hasLeadingZeroBits(submitId(), powBits)
checkLimits(): enforces maxTitleBytes, maxBodyBytes, maxCitations, min/maxPowBits
verifyAll(): checkLimits + verifyPoW + verifySignature

SIMHASH BUCKET CONSTRUCTION (poe_v1_engine.cpp):
  uint64_t sh = e->contentSimhash64()
  Store: poe:v1:simhash:<sidHex>
  Store: poe:v1:contentid:<contentId_hex>
  For band in [0..noveltyBands): bucket key = poe:v1:simbucket:<band>:<2-hex>:<sidHex>
  Bands: up to 16 bands, 4-bit partitions of 64-bit SimHash

ENGINE INITIALIZATION (genesis):
  seed = SHA256('synapsenet_poe_v1_genesis')
  Stored at: meta:poe_v1:seed, meta:poe_v1:version=1
  Persists: entryCount, finalizedCount, epoch_id

VALIDATOR MODE CONFIG:
  validatorMode=stake
  validatorMinStakeAtoms=<threshold>
  Identity/stake records persisted and deterministically resolved

P2P SYNC PROTOCOL:
  poe_getinv / poe_inv (paging inventory, not 'last N')
  inv/getdata → poe_entry / poe_vote / poe_epoch
  Paging ensures full sync even for large entry sets

RPC ENDPOINTS:
  poe.submit, poe.vote, poe.finalize, poe.epoch
  poe.validators, poe.submit_code, poe.list_code, poe.fetch_code
  poe.export, poe.import
  CLI helpers: synapsed poe pubkey, synapsed poe validators

sections.title: Python Sandbox and Bridge
sections.content:
SynapseNet embeds Python for extensibility but with strict security sandboxing.

SECURITY MEASURES:
1. Restricted Imports:
- ALLOWED: math, json, hashlib, typing, dataclasses
- BLOCKED: os, sys, subprocess, socket, ctypes, importlib
- NO file I/O, NO network access, NO system calls

2. Resource Limits:
- Memory: max 50 MB per Python call
- CPU Time: max 1 second per call
- Recursion: max 100 depth

3. Input Validation:
- All data from C++ sanitized before passing to Python
- Python output validated before C++ uses it
- Type checking enforced (no arbitrary objects)

4. Execution Context:
- Python runs in separate thread (not main loop)
- Timeout kills hung scripts
- Errors caught, logged, don't crash node

WHAT PYTHON CAN DO:
✓ Calculate scores
✓ Validate knowledge format
✓ Apply scoring rules
✓ Process structured data

WHAT PYTHON CANNOT DO:
✗ Access filesystem
✗ Make network requests
✗ Execute system commands
✗ Import arbitrary modules

Python files: KeplerSynapseNet/src/python/knowledge_types.py, rules.py, poe.py, scoring.py
Code: KeplerSynapseNet/src/python/python_bridge.cpp, sandbox.cpp

sections.title: PoE Stake Requirements and Anti-Sybil
sections.content:
Stake requirements in SynapseNet serve both anti-spam and anti-Sybil functions.

STAKE REQUIREMENTS:
- Knowledge submission: 10 NGT minimum stake (refundable if accepted, lost if rejected as spam)
- Validation: 100 NGT minimum stake per validator

ANTI-SYBIL DESIGN:
- Stake requirement prevents cheap fake identities (each fake identity needs 100 NGT)
- Random validator selection: cannot choose your friends or coordinate ahead of time
- Reputation builds over time: new accounts have low weight and selection probability
- Rate limiting per address: max X submissions per hour
- Stake-weighted selection (but capped): wealthy attackers can dominate but cost is high

ATTACK VECTORS ON STAKE SYSTEM:
1. COLLUSION: If 3+ validators collude, they can approve garbage.
- Mitigation: random selection from large pool, stake slashing for outlier votes, reputation decay
- Effectiveness: reduces probability, doesn't eliminate

2. STAKE ACCUMULATION: Wealthy attacker buys enough stake to dominate validator pool.
- Mitigation: validator selection weighted by stake but capped; reputation matters more than raw stake over time
- Effectiveness: raises cost; doesn't prevent nation-state actors

3. LLM SPAM FLOOD: AI-generated plausible-looking garbage overwhelms network.
- Mitigation: 10 NGT stake requirement per submission, rate limits, validator training to detect AI content
- Effectiveness: economic barrier; arms race continues

4. SYBIL VALIDATORS: Many fake identities to increase selection probability.
- Mitigation: 100 NGT minimum stake per validator; reputation builds slowly; stake-weighted random selection
- Effectiveness: expensive; not impossible for well-funded attackers

HONEST ASSESSMENT: PoE is a heuristic, not a mathematical proof. It makes attacks expensive, not impossible. Security depends on honest majority: if >50% of stake is malicious, the system fails. Same limitation as Bitcoin's PoW.

sections.title: SynapseNet vs Bitcoin — Detailed Technical Comparison
sections.content:
Detailed comparison between Bitcoin 0.1 (January 2009) and SynapseNet 0.1:

CORE CONCEPT COMPARISON:
| Dimension | Bitcoin | SynapseNet |
|---|---|---|
| Decentralizes | Currency | AI / Knowledge |
| Mining method | Hardware (SHA-256 PoW) | Intelligence (PoE) |
| Created by | Satoshi Nakamoto | Kepler |
| Token | BTC | NGT |
| Consensus | Proof of Work | Proof of Emergence |
| Storage | Blockchain (~850 GB) | Knowledge Chain (~847 GB+ target) |
| Cap | 21 million BTC (hard cap) | No cap (dynamic emission) |
| Quote | Original whitepaper | 'Satoshi gave us money without banks. I will give you brains without corporations.' |

BITCOIN 0.1 CODE STATS (January 2009):
- Total: 31,794 lines of C++ code (one binary: bitcoin.exe)
- GUI (Qt-based): ~15,000 lines, Blockchain core (main.cpp): 2,660 lines
- Networking (net.cpp): 1,020 lines, Cryptography (sha.cpp + key.h): ~1,154 lines
- Dependencies: OpenSSL, MinGW, Qt, Berkeley DB, Boost

SYNAPSENET 0.1 CODE STATS (January 2026):
- Total: 57,897 lines of code (KeplerSynapseNet/{src,include,tests})
- C++: 46,436 lines | Headers: 5,045 lines | Python: 1,184 lines | Tests: 5,232 lines
- ONE BINARY: synapsed (daemon + TUI + CLI)
- src/tui: 10,496 lines | src/core: 6,960 lines | src/main.cpp: 5,476 lines
- src/model: 3,871 | src/web: 3,367 | src/network: 3,461
- WITH ALL (tests + privacy + quantum): 57,897 lines
- Full project (~1 million lines including documentation and all repos)

PHILOSOPHICAL PARALLEL:
- Satoshi: 'The system is secure as long as honest nodes collectively control more CPU power'
- Kepler: 'Secure as long as honest validators collectively control more stake than attackers'
- Both: Transparent about limitations; honest engineering over marketing

faq.a: SynapseNet is a decentralized peer-to-peer network for collective intelligence. It is to knowledge what Bitcoin is to money — nodes mine intelligence instead of hashes, contributors earn NGT tokens, and every local AI benefits from shared community knowledge.

SynapseNet was created by Kepler (GitHub: anakrypt-kepler). Development began in 2023 and was done entirely locally for three years before the first public release in 2026. The project has approximately 1 million lines of code and documentation.

NGT stands for Neural Growth Token. It is the native token of SynapseNet earned by contributing knowledge, validating others' contributions, or renting your AI model. NGT cannot be purchased in-protocol — it can only be earned by protocol outcomes or transferred between wallet addresses.

You earn NGT by: (1) contributing valuable knowledge → base_reward * quality_score; (2) validating others' contributions → small fee per validation; (3) keeping your node online → passive rewards; (4) renting your AI model to others (PAID mode) → NGT per hour. Early participants earn more due to the dynamic emission model.

Proof of Emergence is SynapseNet's consensus mechanism for validating knowledge contributions. Unlike Proof of Work (burn energy) or Proof of Stake (lock capital), PoE rewards useful knowledge. It uses: Hashcash-style PoW as anti-spam gate, deterministic SimHash novelty check, random validator selection, M-of-N voting (3-of-5), and epoch-based citation graph rewards.

No. NGT cannot be purchased in-protocol. It can only be earned through protocol outcomes (knowledge contributions, validation, model rental) or transferred between wallet addresses. This is an immutable creator rule.

NAAN stands for Node-Attached Autonomous Agent Network. When you run a SynapseNet node, an attached background agent automatically starts. This agent autonomously researches topics, drafts knowledge entries, validates others' submissions, and earns NGT rewards. You cannot disable it while the node runs — to stop it, exit synapsed.

Yes, SynapseNet has multiple privacy modes: --privacy (Tor + E2E + scrubbed logs), --privacy --stealth (+ stealth addresses + Dandelion++), --amnesia (RAM-only, zero disk traces), --hidden-wallet (plausible deniability). However, perfect privacy is NOT guaranteed — global adversaries monitoring all Tor traffic may still correlate patterns. Anonymity is a spectrum, not a switch.

faq.q: What AI models does SynapseNet support?
faq.a: SynapseNet supports GGUF format models (llama.cpp compatible). The default model is Llama 2 7B Chat (Q2_K, ~2.6 GB). You can also use Llama 3.x, Mistral 7B, Phi-2, Phi-3, CodeLlama, or your own fine-tuned models. ONNX, SafeTensors, and PyTorch formats are planned. Models are stored in ~/.synapsenet/models/.

The Knowledge Chain is SynapseNet's distributed database of human knowledge. It is like a blockchain but stores intelligence instead of transactions. Design target: ~847 GB (similar to Bitcoin's ~850 GB). Each node stores a fair share (~65 MB at design scale). Knowledge is automatically replicated to 12+ nodes and cannot be deleted by any single actor.

Yes. SynapseNet uses Tor by default (agent.tor.required=true). All NAAN research, P2P connections, and web searches route through Tor SOCKS5. Supports obfs4 bridges for censored networks. Runs as fail-closed: if Tor is unavailable, traffic is blocked rather than falling back to clearnet.

DHT/Kademlia peer discovery is deferred. No implementation exists in the current source tree. SynapseNet currently uses DNS seeds, bootstrap nodes, and peer exchange (GETPEERS/PEERS) for peer discovery, which covers current requirements. DHT may be added when the network grows beyond what static seeds can handle.

SynapseNet implements a multi-layer quantum security stack. Layer 0: Classic (Ed25519, AES-256-GCM, X25519). Layer 1: Lattice-based PQC (ML-KEM-768/Kyber, ML-DSA-65/Dilithium). Layer 2: Hash-based PQC (SPHINCS+). Layer 3: OTP. Layer 4: QKD (future). Quantum security requires the liboqs library — without it, PQC runs in simulation mode which is clearly disclosed in node.status.

Build: cmake -S KeplerSynapseNet -B KeplerSynapseNet/build -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release -DBUILD_TESTS=ON && cmake --build KeplerSynapseNet/build --parallel 8. Run: TERM=xterm-256color ./KeplerSynapseNet/build/synapsed. Docker: docker run --rm -it -p 8332:8332 keplersynapsenet:local.

SynapseIDE is SynapseNet's coding environment. It has two surfaces: (1) terminal IDE (synapseide) built in Go, featuring isolated threads (/tangent), unified diff patch mode (/patch), and GitHub Quests; (2) VS Code extension (ide/synapsenet-vscode) with inline AI completions, chat panel, and GitHub Quests workflow. In v0.1.0-alphaV4, the Go terminal IDE is being replaced with a Tauri desktop app.

You can fork the code, but any fork that changes core behavior, removes safeguards, or bypasses prohibitions is NOT an official SynapseNet project. NGT earned by such a fork is not recognized or associated with SynapseNet. Wallets tied to such violations may be subject to automatic burn by community decision.

alphaV4 is a major architectural migration that: eliminates Go from the project entirely, rewrites all Go components (synapseide terminal IDE) in C++, replaces the bubbletea terminal TUI with a Tauri desktop application (TypeScript + Svelte + Monaco Editor), exposes synapsed as libsynapsed.so/dylib/dll for Tauri FFI, and uses one build system (CMake + Cargo) instead of two (CMake + go build).

faq.q: What cryptographic primitives does SynapseNet use?
faq.a: Classic: SHA-256, Ed25519 (signatures), AES-256-GCM (encryption), X25519 (key exchange), secp256k1 (Bitcoin-compatible). Post-quantum: CRYSTALS-Kyber/ML-KEM-768 (KEM), CRYSTALS-Dilithium/ML-DSA-65 (signatures), SPHINCS+/SLH-DSA-128s (hash-based signatures). OTP: HKDF-based deterministic one-time pad. QKD: BB84 protocol (future). Hashing: SHA-256 for all content IDs and PoW.

Web 4.0 is SynapseNet's vision: you talk to a local agent (a model you own, running on your hardware). The agent optionally fetches context from clearnet and onion sources, cross-checks it, extracts signal, and returns a synthesized answer with citations. This is not 'search' — it is a digital cortex: an extension of cognition that lives on your machine, not on someone else's API. The primary currencies in Web 4.0 are inference (compute you provide or rent) and useful knowledge (Proof of Emergence).

No. As of March 2026, SynapseNet is in active alpha development (v0.1.0-alphaV4). All network statistics, knowledge chain sizes (~847 GB), and node counts in the documentation are visualizations of how the system is designed to operate at scale — they are modeled projections, not live metrics from a running public network. The website synapsenetai.org is currently offline.

faq.q: What is the NGT token cap?
faq.a: NGT has NO hard cap. Unlike Bitcoin's 21 million limit, NGT uses a dynamic emission model: emission_rate = base_rate / (1 + network_load_factor). More network activity means less NGT per action. Scarcity comes from demand, not artificial limits. The network should grow forever — knowledge is infinite — so new contributors should always be incentivized.

Validators are selected deterministically using: validators = PRF(prevBlockHash, entryId) → choose N validators. This uses a pseudorandom function seeded by the previous block hash (unpredictable ahead of time) and the entry's unique ID. Default: 5 validators per entry, requiring 3-of-5 to finalize. Minimum stake for validators: 100 NGT. The algorithm is deterministic — all nodes compute the same validator set for any given entry.

faq.q: What languages is SynapseNet written in?
faq.a: SynapseNet v0.1.0-alphaV3: C++ (node daemon synapsed), Go (synapseide terminal IDE), TypeScript/JS (VS Code extension), Python (sandboxed scoring/rules). Build: CMake (C++), go build (Go). In v0.1.0-alphaV4, Go is being eliminated — the Go IDE is rewritten in C++ and wrapped with a Tauri (Rust + TypeScript/Svelte) desktop app. Final stack: C++ + Rust (Tauri) + TypeScript/Svelte.

faq.q: How many tests does SynapseNet have?
faq.a: As of February 23, 2026: 230/230 tests passing (ctest --test-dir KeplerSynapseNet/build --output-on-failure). The test suite covers: PoE consensus, NAAN agent runtime/scheduler/score/coordination/pipeline, wallet/crypto, quantum security, network/scale/discovery, privacy, Tor routing/bridges, TUI navigation, update manifest/installer, implant compatibility, and determinism replay. Located in KeplerSynapseNet/tests/ (67+ test files).
